topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a monotonous soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I watched Sarah and Mark walk toward Mr. Henderson’s office, their shared smiles radiating an easy camaraderie that felt deliberately exclusionary. When Mr. Henderson finally called us in separately, his tone was measured, almost patronizing. "Both of you have demonstrated exceptional aptitude for the Senior Analyst role," he began, tapping a pen against the file. My jaw tightened. I kept my expression carefully neutral, forcing a polite nod, but inside, a hot flush crept up my neck. I felt a sudden, sharp clenching in my chest, like a fist squeezing too tightly. When he announced the decision, I simply nodded again, the movement stiff. The silence in the room stretched, thick and suffocating, as I gathered my things, the click of my briefcase closing sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet aftermath.

[story 2]

I stood by the water cooler, pretending to examine the condensation on the plastic bottle, while listening to the hushed, triumphant exchange between David and Emily. They were practically beaming, their shared victory palpable even from across the breakroom. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the counter. Every casual word they exchanged—a shared joke, a congratulatory gesture—felt like a deliberate slight. I forced a slow sip of water, trying to regulate the rapid thumping beneath my ribs. When the announcement came, it landed like a physical blow. My gaze fixed on a point just past their heads, refusing to meet anyone’s eyes. A strange, cold numbness began to spread through my limbs, a defensive shield against the sudden, intense heat rising in my face. I simply murmured, "Congratulations," the words tasting like ash in my mouth.

[story 3]

The air in the conference room felt thin, pressurized. I could feel the weight of everyone’s expectation pressing down on my shoulders. When the discussion shifted to the promotion candidates, the atmosphere instantly crystallized around Sarah and Mark. I watched their interaction—the way they leaned in, the subtle, knowing glances—and a distinct tension coiled low in my stomach. I shifted in my seat, adjusting my posture until I was rigid, every muscle taut. When the final decision was delivered, my breath hitched, a shallow, involuntary intake of air. I managed a strained smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes. Later, as I walked back to my desk, I kept my pace even, acutely aware of the way people subtly steered clear of me, the lingering residue of that shared moment leaving a bitter, sour taste in my mouth.
